case_name: Bromall v. Select Specialty Hosp. Akron, L.L.C.

summary.brief: Bromall v. Select Specialty Hosp. Akron, L.L.C.. Medical malpractice case involving cardiology.

evidence_index.text: On March 4, 2016, Paul S. Bromall (“Bromall”), age 62, was admitted to Akron General Health Systems (“Akron General”) where he was treated for an infected ulcer on his ankle.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Bromall had an extensive medical history, including diagnoses of “chronic kidney disease stage 4 secondary to diabetic nephropathy, diabetes mellitus type 2, hypertension, secondary hyperparathyroidism, anemia in chronic kidney disease, and morbid obesity.”
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On March 28, 2016, Dr. Al-Yafi removed Bromall’s sutures from the catheter incision site.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The following morning, a registered nurse discovered Bromall sitting up in bed with his dialysis catheter lying on the floor next to him.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On March 30, 2019, Bromall’s potassium level rose to 6.0 mmol/L.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Blood drawn at 3:52 a.m. revealed that Bromall had a potassium level greater than 9.0 mmol/L.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Huntley therefore concluded that “this was a preventable death that could have been
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Huntley

evidence_index.text: Dr. Brown similarly opined that “[Dr. Al-Yafi] was negligent in his
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Brown

evidence_index.text: The probative value of the statements by Dr. Raina that Dr. Al-Yafi was responsible for Bromall’s death substantially outweighed the danger of unfair prejudice, confusion of the issues, or misleading the jury.
evidence_index.speaker: Court analysis

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Admitted for treatment of an infected ulcer on the ankle. Extensive medical history including chronic kidney disease stage 4, diabetes mellitus type 2, hypertension, secondary hyperparathyroidism, anemia in chronic kidney disease, and morbid obesity.

focused_exam.general: Patient with multiple comorbidities, admitted for infected ulcer treatment

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Infected ulcer with underlying chronic kidney disease

reveal.result_summary: Potassium level rose to 6.0 mmol/L on March 30, 2019
reveal.clinical_significance: Indicates hyperkalemia, which is dangerous in patients with renal failure
reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt urgent management of hyperkalemia

reveal.result_summary: Potassium level greater than 9.0 mmol/L at 3:52 a.m. on March 31, 2016
reveal.clinical_significance: Critically high potassium level, associated with cardiac arrest

timeline_phases.name: Initial Hospital Admission
timeline_phases.description: Patient admitted for infected ulcer treatment

key_events.event: Admission to Akron General Health Systems

timeline_phases.name: Dialysis and Catheter Management
timeline_phases.description: Management of dialysis and catheter issues

timeline_phases.name: Critical Event and Outcome
timeline_phases.description: Patient found unresponsive and subsequent death

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with rising potassium levels and no dialysis access
decision_points.prompt: What should be done to manage the patient's hyperkalemia?

options.description: Initiate urgent management for hyperkalemia, including temporary dialysis
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard care for hyperkalemia in patients with renal failure

options.description: Continue with planned catheter replacement without urgent intervention
options.clinical_reasoning: Assumed potassium levels would stabilize without intervention

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant did not employ urgent management treatments for hyperkalemia
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Believed potassium level was not at a critical range

expected_action_court.description: Urgent management for hyperkalemia should have been initiated
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established urgent management was necessary

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to manage hyperkalemia led to cardiac arrest
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Urgent management should have been initiated
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient died due to complications from hyperkalemia

key_findings: Court found no abuse of discretion in excluding Dr. Raina's statements
Expert testimony provided alternative evidence on standard of care

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to administer emergency treatment for hyperkalemia
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Immediate intervention could have prevented death

factual_timeline.event: Patient admitted to Akron General Health Systems
Patient found unresponsive and pronounced dead
Patient admitted to Akron General Health Systems

treatments.outcome: Not completed due to patient's death

allegations.allegation: Failure to administer emergency treatment for hyperkalemia

court_findings.finding: No breach of standard of care by Dr. Al-Yafi
court_findings.legal_significance: Jury verdict in favor of defendants

court_findings.finding: No abuse of discretion in excluding Dr. Raina's statements
court_findings.legal_significance: Court upheld exclusion of speculative opinions
